title: Verify watchlist persists across app restarts
description: WatchlistStore writes through a SwiftData ModelContext (context.save() on add/remove/reorder) and StockAlertsApp constructs the container with the default initializer: `ModelContainer(for: WatchedSymbol.self, PriceAlert.self)`, which should persist on-disk to Application Support. However, there is no test verifying the on-disk round-trip — all existing WatchlistStoreTests use TestHelpers.makeInMemoryContainer(). We need to confirm (and guard with a regression test) that a WatchedSymbol inserted during one app launch is still there on the next launch.
design:
Add a WatchlistStorePersistenceTests file under StockAlertsTests/ that:
1. Creates a temp directory URL for the store file (FileManager.default.temporaryDirectory.appendingPathComponent(UUID()...).
2. Builds container A with ModelConfiguration(url: tempURL), WatchlistStore A, adds symbols, saves, releases both.
3. Builds container B with the same URL and asserts WatchlistStore B sees the symbols.
4. Remove the temp file in tearDown.

Remember the ModelContext lifetime gotcha (CLAUDE.md): the test must retain the ModelContainer for the duration of each step — don't just keep the context.

No production code changes are expected unless the test reveals a real bug in StockAlertsApp.init() container construction.
acceptance_criteria:
- Manual smoke test: launch app, add AAPL + MSFT to the watchlist, quit the app (Cmd-Q), relaunch — both tickers reappear in the sidebar in the same order.
- Automated test exercising the real on-disk container at a test-scoped URL (ModelConfiguration(url:)), writing with one container instance and reading with a fresh one. Pattern should mirror the subprocess test idea but may stay in-process if a separate ModelContainer over the same .store URL is enough to simulate relaunch.
- If the test fails, fix root cause (likely a ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly:) or a sandbox-container URL issue); do not simply add a shim.
close_reason: Automated test WatchlistStorePersistenceTests.symbolsPersist_acrossContainerInstances_onDisk added and passing. Verified on-disk round-trip: a WatchedSymbol written via one ModelContainer is readable via a fresh ModelContainer at the same store URL. No production change required — default ModelContainer init already persists to Application Support. Manual Cmd-Q smoke test still pending in the live app.

title: Open Apple Stocks.app for a watched ticker
description:
Currently a user can see quotes in StockAlerts but cannot jump to Apple's built-in Stocks.app for a richer chart and news feed. Add an affordance that launches Stocks.app, ideally pre-scoped to the selected ticker.

Plausible entry points (pick one — don't build all three):
1. Context-menu item 'Open in Stocks' on each sidebar row in MainWindowView.
2. 'Open in Stocks' button in SymbolDetailView's header toolbar.
3. 'Open in Stocks' on each row in the MenuBarPopoverView list.
design:
Apple's Stocks.app on macOS handles the 'stocks://' URL scheme and accepts symbol deep-links (e.g., 'stocks://?symbol=AAPL' — verify current behaviour on the target macOS before shipping; Apple has changed this over the years).

Sketch:
1. Thin helper StocksAppLauncher with a single @MainActor static func open(symbol: String) that builds the URL and calls NSWorkspace.shared.open. Inject a seam (protocol / closure) for tests so we never actually launch Stocks.app in CI.
2. Pick the context-menu entry point (option 1 above) to start — minimal UI surface, easy to reach.
3. Add a SwiftUI-level smoke test (follow the 'only exception: SwiftUI views' rule in CLAUDE.md — manual test in the running app, document the click path in the PR).
acceptance_criteria:
- Invoking the action for ticker AAPL launches Stocks.app (bringing it frontmost) and, if the Stocks.app URL scheme supports it, lands on AAPL's symbol page.
- If Stocks.app is not installed (e.g., an older macOS or a stripped image), the action is either hidden or surfaces a graceful non-fatal error — do not crash.
- Action stays on the main actor; uses NSWorkspace.open(_:configuration:completionHandler:) rather than blocking calls.
